1: ὁ δέσμιος τοῦ Χριστοῦ Ἰησοῦ (the prisoner of Christ Jesus) — the genitive is possessive: Paul belongs to Christ in his imprisonment, not merely to Caesar. The same designation returns at Ephesians 4:1. The interruption — Paul begins "for this reason I, Paul…" then digresses through Ephesians 3:13 before completing the sentence at Ephesians 3:14 — is structurally deliberate: the digression explains why the imprisonment is not grounds for discouragement.
2: οἰκονομίαν τῆς χάριτος (stewardship/administration of grace — oikonomia) — the household-management term; Paul is the appointed manager of a trust. The grace is not his to generate but to distribute. The phrase δοθείσης μοι εἰς ὑμᾶς (given to me for you) marks the directional purpose: the grace-stewardship is apostolically oriented toward the Gentiles.
3: κατὰ ἀποκάλυψιν (by/according to revelation) — the knowledge came by divine disclosure, not human investigation. This is consistent with Paul's claim in Galatians 1:12. τοῦ μυστηρίου — the mystery he has written briefly about; the reader is expected to recognize it. ἐγνωρίσθη (was made known — aorist passive) positions Paul as recipient, not discoverer.
4: μυστήριον τοῦ Χριστοῦ (mystery of Christ — objective genitive: the mystery that concerns/is about Christ) — not a secret teaching Christ possesses but the previously hidden divine plan now disclosed in Christ. Reading (ἀναγινώσκοντες, present participle) is assumed to be public, communal — the letter is being read aloud in assembly. The comprehension (νοῆσαι, to perceive/understand) is the intended result.
5: οὐκ ἐγνωρίσθη (was not made known — aorist passive) establishes a contrast with νῦν ἀπεκαλύφθη (now has been revealed — aorist passive). The stress is on the difference between former hiddenness and present disclosure, not absolute ignorance in earlier generations. ἀποστόλοις καὶ προφήταις (apostles and prophets — dative of disclosure) — NT apostles and prophets, not the OT prophets, receive the revelation here described.
6: Three syn- compound nouns, each a NT hapax or near-hapax: συγκληρονόμα (co-heirs), σύσσωμα (members of the same body — unique compound), συμμέτοχα (co-participants/fellow-partakers). The accumulation is literary emphasis through structural parallelism: the Gentiles are fully included, not secondarily attached. All three terms describe the Gentiles in relation to what Israel already had in promise.
7: κατὰ τὴν ἐνέργειαν τῆς δυνάμεως αὐτοῦ (according to the working of his power) — the same phrase as in Ephesians 1:19. The grace given for the ministry is not Paul's native capacity but God's effective power operating through him. διάκονος (servant/minister) is the self-designation — not lord or patron but one who serves at table; the term underscores the subordinate, commissioned character of apostolic ministry.
8: ἐλαχιστοτέρῳ (lesser than the least) — a double comparative grammatically irregular and deliberately excessive: the comparative of the superlative elachistos. Paul names himself beneath even the category of "least." τοῖς ἔθνεσιν εὐαγγελίσασθαι τὸν ἀνεξιχνίαστον πλοῦτον τοῦ Χριστοῦ — ἀνεξιχνίαστον (unsearchable/untraceable — beyond investigation by following the tracks) positions the riches of Christ as inexhaustible and beyond calculation.
9: φωτίσαι (to illuminate/enlighten — aorist infinitive of purpose) — the second purpose of Paul's ministry: making visible what was hidden. ἀποκεκρυμμένου (having been hidden — perfect passive participle) marks the hiddenness as a completed prior state now overcome. τοῦ θεοῦ τοῦ τὰ πάντα κτίσαντος (of God who created all things) — the creator of all things is the same one who hid and now discloses the mystery; creation and redemption share one agent.
10: πολυποίκιλος σοφία (manifold/many-colored wisdom — polypoikilos is NT hapax) — the adjective is applied in classical sources to embroidered cloth with many patterns; God's wisdom displayed through the church is variegated, not monochrome. ταῖς ἀρχαῖς καὶ ταῖς ἐξουσίαις (to the rulers and authorities in the heavenly realms) — the church is the instrument by which angelic/cosmic powers are shown God's wisdom. The church's existence is itself a cosmic announcement.
11: κατὰ πρόθεσιν τῶν αἰώνων (according to the purpose of the ages — literally "the eternal purpose") — aiōnōn (of the ages) functions as an intensive genitive expressing permanence; God's purpose spans and transcends all ages. ἐν τῷ Χριστῷ Ἰησοῦ τῷ κυρίῳ ἡμῶν — the full title marks the sentence's doxological weight; the eternal purpose is located in, and accomplished in, this specific person.
12: παρρησίαν καὶ προσαγωγήν (boldness and access) — the two combined describe the worshipper's standing before God. παρρησία (parrēsia) in Greek culture meant freedom of speech, the right to speak openly without fear — applied here to prayer. προσαγωγή (access, right of approach — same word as Ephesians 2:18) pairs freedom of speech with the right of entry. Both are ἐν πεποιθήσει (with confidence — not anxious or tentative).
13: αἰτοῦμαι μὴ ἐγκακεῖν (I ask not to lose heart/faint) — Paul requests that the Ephesians not be discouraged by his sufferings on their behalf. ἐγκακεῖν (to faint, grow weary, lose courage — present infinitive) describes a process of giving up. ἥτις ἐστὶν δόξα ὑμῶν (which is your glory) — the suffering is reframed not as shame but as honor accruing to them; his chains are a mark of his advocacy for them.
14: κάμπτω τὰ γόνατά μου (I bend my knees) — explicit posture of prayer; Jewish practice normally involved standing (1 Kings 8:22), making kneeling a mark of particular intensity or urgency (Luke 22:41; Acts 20:36). The posture embodies the petition's seriousness. The resumption of the interrupted sentence from Ephesians 3:1 signals the prayer is the climax toward which the entire parenthetical section (Ephesians 3:2-13) pointed.
15: ἐξ οὗ πᾶσα πατριά (from whom every family) — the wordplay between πατριά (family/clan, from patēr) and πατήρ (Father) is deliberate: God the Father is the source from whom all fatherhood and family-identity derives its name. The claim is not merely that God is a father figure but that the very concept of family-naming originates in him — a polemical claim against Roman imperial family ideology.
16: κατὰ τὸν πλοῦτον τῆς δόξης αὐτοῦ (according to the riches of his glory) — the measure of the gift is not human need but divine resource. κραταιωθῆναι (to be strengthened — aorist passive infinitive) is a strong compound: empowered with might. The target: εἰς τὸν ἔσω ἄνθρωπον (into the inner person/man) — the inner self, the willing center, the seat of moral agency (cf. Romans 7:22; 2 Corinthians 4:16). The Spirit's strengthening reaches the deepest interior.
17: κατοικῆσαι (katoikēsai, to dwell/settle permanently — aorist infinitive) is contrasted with paroikēsai (to sojourn temporarily). The request is for Christ's permanent, not transient, dwelling in the heart — an indwelling that is household-establishment, not a visit. ἐρριζωμένοι καὶ τεθεμελιωμένοι (rooted and established — perfect participles) mix botanical and architectural metaphors; both communicate deep, irreversible anchorage in love as the foundation.
18: τί τὸ πλάτος καὶ μῆκος καὶ ὕψος καὶ βάθος (what is the breadth and length and height and depth) — four spatial dimensions applied to the love of Christ. The fourfold measure is a conventional device for totality (north, south, east, west; all directions); here it signals that Christ's love exceeds any one orientation. The object of comprehension is specified in Ephesians 3:19: the love that surpasses knowledge — a paradox the prayer sustains rather than resolves.
19: γνῶναι τε τὴν ὑπερβάλλουσαν τῆς γνώσεως ἀγάπην (to know the love of Christ that surpasses knowledge) — the grammatical structure is intentionally paradoxical: to know the unknowable. Hyperballousa (surpassing — present participle, ongoing excess) marks the love as perpetually beyond the knower's grasp even while it is known. πληρωθῆτε εἰς πᾶν τὸ πλήρωμα τοῦ θεοῦ (be filled to all the fullness of God) — the passive voice; God is the filler, believers are filled; the corporate "you" (plural) receives the fullness.
20: ὑπερεκπερισσοῦ (immeasurably/abundantly beyond — triple-compound superlative, NT hapax) stacks intensifiers: hyper (above) + ek (out of) + perissos (abundant). No English renders it adequately. κατὰ τὴν δύναμιν τὴν ἐνεργουμένην ἐν ἡμῖν (according to the power working in us — present passive participle, ongoing) — the criterion for God's ability to exceed human imagination is not his abstract omnipotence but the power already actively working within the community.
21: The doxology closes the first half of Ephesians with matched recipients: αὐτῷ ἡ δόξα ἐν τῇ ἐκκλησίᾳ καὶ ἐν Χριστῷ Ἰησοῦ (to him be glory in the church and in Christ Jesus). The church and Christ are the twin spheres of divine glory-display — the heavenly and the embodied. εἰς πάσας τὰς γενεὰς τοῦ αἰῶνος τῶν αἰώνων (to all the generations of the age of ages) — a heaping of temporal expressions for eternity. Ἀμήν seals the doxology as community affirmation.
